EXT. THE HIMALAYAS

Wide as all the world. Towering and timeless and rumbling mutely into the sky to scrape the floor of heaven. We're GLIDING BETWEEN them as if on a cloud, and CARTER'S VOICE is quiet and humble and yet somehow makes us feel as though he knows a great many things we don't.
Edward Perriman Cole died in May. It was a Sunday afternoon, and there wasn't a cloud in the sky...
One mountain rises above the rest. A plume of ice and snow billows from its wedge-shaped peak which thrusts up into the jet stream.

It's difficult to understand the sum of a person's life... Some people will tell you it's measured by the ones left behind. Some believe it can be measured in faith. Some say by love... Other folks say life has no meaning at all. We're MOVING HIGHER now as the mountain looms closer,
drawing us UP its massive shoulders.
Me, I believe that you measure yourself by the people who measure themselves by you.
CONTINUING OVER ridges and glaciers towards the peak.
EXT. MOUNT EVEREST

A SKI POLE THRUSTING THROUGH A CRUST OF FROZEN SNOW. The CLIMBER is wrapped in a hooded mountaineering suit and his face is protected by an oxygen mask and ski goggles. With great effort, he lifts his right foot and plants it forward next to the ski pole, his thin breath swept away by the exertion as he turns to take in the view of the entire world beneath him...
What I can tell you for sure is that by any measure, Edward Cole lived more in his last days on earth than most folks manage to wring out of a lifetime.
The climber turns back to the summit.
I know that when he died his eyes were closed, and his heart was open... And I know that he could hear the mountain...
The climber plants the second ski pole and takes another agonizing step as we RISE HIGH ABOVE him until he becomes small on the face of the giant.
And I know the mountain heard him in return.
And we slowly... ASHING INTO AN EMPTY "CHOCK FULL O' NUTS" COFFEE CAN.
Name the five Presidents whose last names begin with the letter 'H.'
INT. McCREATH SERVICE CENTER - DAY

CARTER CHAMBERS (66) is a black mechanic with a worn, but thoughtful demeanor. He leans back against the El Camino he is working on. His partner MANNY (26) is beneath the hood, working on the engine. Next to him is a book of Trivia.
Warren G. Harding, Rutherford B.
Ah, I thought I'd get you with the double Harrisons. You're sick, you know that.
You think that's sick? name their wives?
No. Neither can I.
From across the shop:
Yesterday.
(reading from the book) All right, I got one for you. invented the radio?
Now that's a tough one.
What do you know, I finally got you.
No, I just don't know if you're looking for the guy who everyone thinks invented the radio or the guy who actually invented it.
It says here...
Marconi, right? Yeah.
It ain't him? (2)

Well, he's generally accepted as the inventor. In fact, he won the Nobel Prize for it in 1909. The truth is a man by the name of Nikola Tesla patented the basic system of radio in 1896, a system Marconi used to get his own patent several years later.
The PHONE RINGS. As Carter ambles towards it, where it hangs on the wall next to a calendar of vintage Ford Mustangs:
Tesla fought Marconi until the day he died in 1943. That same year, the Supreme Court held Marconi's patent invalid, recognizing Nikola Tesla as the primary inventor of radio.
You really are sick.
Carter picks up the phone.
(into phone) This is Carter... Oh, hey, Ginnie... Yep... About time. What did he say?... Uh-huh... What does that mean?... Okay, then.
He quietly hangs up the phone and removes the butt from between his lips as smoke dribbles from his mouth. He looks at the cigarette for a moment, then drops it to the
floor.
He steps on the cigarette as he walks in a daze past Manny and out the door of the garage.
INT. RIVERSIDE COUNTY BOARD OF SUPERVISORS - CHAMBER DAY
ANGLE ON A HAND-HAMMERED COPPER THERMOS releasing a waft of rich steam as the dark contents are poured into a cup which is lifted to the waiting nostrils of EDWARD COLE who inhales deeply.
THE BUCKET LIST - Rev. 12/4/06
Kopi Luwak. The rarest beverage in the world.

Sitting on the dais, the BOARD CHAIRMAN who's flanked by four fellow BOARD MEMBERS who are moving to their seats. At an adjacent table are representatives of the county health department and Winwood Hospital administrators. Edward is in his mid-60s. He's dressed casually almost in defiance of the occasion. He hands the cup over to RICHARD, one of several EXECUTIVES in Edward's retinue.
(coaxing with his hand) Here, take a whiff.
Richard bends his head to the cup, sniffing it. Mister Cole?
We're ready to begin.
Hold on a sec.

Edward nods for Richard to take a sip. Realizing he has to take a sip in order for the meeting to proceed, Richard takes a tentative sip. Edward's eyes bore into Richard's, waiting for a response. Well?
(clearly doesn't get it) Really good.
(to Richard) 'Really good.'
Mister Cole... sir? (2)
You know what a Philistine is, Jim?
Sir, it's Richard.
(coughing) That's right, Phil. Give them the schpiel.
Richard takes the podium.

Thank you, Mister Chairman, fellow supervisors. Thank you for allowing us the opportunity to address you today. We at the Cole group feel that the decline of Winwood Hospital is a direct result of significant fiscal mismanagement. I beg your pardon! This -RICHARD Overhead costs are nearly double what they should be. Your lease and land costs were similarly overbid. Donations have atrophied to the point where you've fallen well behind the curve in research, pediatrics, oncology, and MRI.
Edward COUGHS again as he watches the proceedings with seeming disinterest. The administrators range from ashamed to apoplectic.

The Cole Group has successfully privatized fifteen public hospitals in the past seven years, each of which now provides, or is being re-designed to provide, the highest standard of medical care to their communities.
Despite being grossly understaffed?
The better the doctors, the less need there is for -- What about beds? There have been reports that you increase the number of patients to the point of overpopulation.
Patient density has always been... And your emergency rooms are known to be --
Edward abruptly stands.

I run hospitals, not health spas. Two beds to a room. No exceptions. Look, I passed up a lunch with Michelle Pfeiffer to be here. So can we desist from further inane posturing. You need me, boys and girls... I don't need you. Now, there's a sizeable check in this envelope. Let me know if you decide to cash it.
Coughs into his handkerchief again. Mister Cole?
BOARD CHAIRMAN
Edward stares in shock at the ugly puddle of clotted blood staining the white silk of the handkerchief...
INT. GREYHALL MEMORIAL ONCOLOGY CENTER - DAY A pair of elevator doors open at the far end of a long
hallway emitting THOMAS, mid-30s, lugging a set of two massive suitcases and a bunch of flowers tucked under his arm as he counts room numbers.
INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - DAY

Thomas barrels in and drops the bags onto the empty bed by the door. He opens the first suitcase, beginning to set up the room with practiced efficiency... He plugs in a cell phone cradle and sets it on the bedside table along with the laptop computer...
THE BUCKET LIST - Rev. 12/4/06

He places a Royal Classic copper coffee siphon on the window sill, along with a copper Thermos and saucers and spoons, and an elegant wooden box. Finally he removes a crystal vase and stuffs the flowers into it. VOICES are heard in the hallway -- a commotion drawing closer. Anticipating their arrival, Thomas, looking for a place for the flowers, quickly walks around the bed and yanks open the separator curtain which bisects the room, revealing Carter.
What are you doing here?

Carter is thin and drawn and plugged into a network of tubes. His bedside table is stacked high with an imposing tower of books. The wall behind him is collaged with get well cards and pictures of his family: two sons, several grandchildren and a beautiful 20-year-old daughter.
Oh, you know, fighting for my life... You?
No, I was just surprised that...
Just as the hallway brigade bursts into the room as doctors and nurses and handlers hover over -EDWARD
That's not exactly what -EDWARD Where the hell is Thomas?
In plain view, sir. We're going to move you into the bed now.
I can do it myself, goddammit. ain't dead yet.
He tries to lift himself into the bed, but falls to the floor.
Have I fired you lately?
Not since the Oprah interview.
He bends down and helps Edward into the bed. settles a moment before noticing Carter.
Who the hell is that?
Who the hell are you?
That was the first time I laid eyes on Edward Cole. An inauspicious beginning to be sure.
INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - EARLY MORNING
ELECTRIC CLIPPERS SHAVING SMOOTH AVENUES ONTO EDWARD'S SCALP. Edward's mood has not improved.
I'll be damned if I'm gonna spend the next three weeks lying next to this guy. He looks half dead already.
You can't have your own room. It would create enormous PR problems.
I don't give a shit about PR. I want my own room. It's my hospital, for Christ's sake. Don't tell me I can't have my own room. (to Carter) No offense, pal.
You have defended this policy publicly countless times. You run hospitals, not health spas. Two beds to a room. No exceptions.
I never been sick before.
NURSE SHING squeezes a small dollop of cream onto Edward's pate.
She walks out, leaving him to stare in horror at his newly-bald reflection in the mirror... Jesus.
(to Thomas) Thomas, don't let me wake up paralyzed.
I'll do what I can.
(after a beat) This really your hospital?
Yeah, pretty much.
Might wanna do something about the pea soup.
THE BUCKET LIST - Rev. 12/4/06 INT. OPERATING THEATER - DAY
Dr. Hollins and his surgical team operate on Edward who lies in fragile stasis.

By the morning of the surgery, the cancer had spread so far throughout Edward's body that the doctors gave him only a five percent chance to survive... but then they didn't account for how pissed off they'd made him.
PUSH INTO Edward's inert face which, even under sedation, appears annoyed at the intrusion. From the operating lights we...
INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - NIGHT
Edward lies quietly, eyes closed. Carter is sitting up in bed eating a home-cooked meal brought by his wife VIRGINIA (mid-60s), who sits in a chair beside his bed. No visitors come in to see him?
(shakes his head) He's been sleeping a lot since they brought him back.
That's another reason I don't miss nursing... Always so sad seeing a patient like that all alone after that kind of surgery.
(nods sympathetically) 'Least it's quiet.
Virginia's unsure if he's being editorial about her presence. They sit in silence for several moments. She looks at him as if expecting him to speak. Her discomfort is palpable.
Rachel called this morning.
How is she doing?
She's auditioning for first violin in next semester's symphony.
That's wonderful.
Another beat... Finally, she starts to collect her things.
You need any more books? Nah, I'm good.
Nods at the healthy stack of tomes on his bedside table.
Already took 'em. How about pillows?
I'm fine... Thank you, Virginia.
(forcing a smile)
A beat as she girds herself, working up to -VIRGINIA I can stay awhile if... She takes his hand...
No sense in both of us being useless in the morning. Okay.

She pulls her hand away, then leans over to kiss him, but the closeness is awkward. Her eyes fall, feeling the lack as they touch cheeks. She squeezes his hand then turns to go, disappearing through the door. He stares at the empty doorway for several moments.
(eyes still closed) She gone? What?
Edward opens his eyes for the first time, looks over at Carter.
As something of a public health expert, it's my belief more people die from visitors than disease.
Edward and Carter exchange looks.
What is a BlackBerry?
INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - DAY
Edward opens his eyes to see Carter's television turned on to "Jeopardy."
(on TV) This Swedish berry is also known as the cowberry.
What is a lingonberry?
(on TV) I'll take It's the Berries for eight hundred, Alex.
Edward, who's in some pain, watches with an "I can't believe I'm stuck in a room with this guy" expression.
(on TV) In a top 40 hit of 1956, this 'Berry' told Beethoven to roll over.
Who is Chuck Berry?
Hey, pal, do you mind...?
He indicates lowering the volume.
Oh. Sorry. (lowers the volume to the TV) What is The Mariana Trench? Huh?
Just as Dr. HOLLINS enters.
Morning, Edward. How you feeling? Dumb question.
Dr. Hollins nods approvingly. Marks the chart.
How's the catheter?
Love it. I don't know how I ever did without it. Good.
Humor is a healthy sign. Kiss my ass.
As is surliness.
He checks the integrity of the bulging plastic Groshong catheter implanted several inches above Edward's heart.
The operation went well. All the post-op brain scans were clean. Now we go after the sarcoma in the rest of your body. (MORE)
Unfortunately, your blood markers are extremely high, so we'll need to begin the first course of chemo this morning.
I love the smell of chemo in the morning.
I'll be back to check on you later.
Dr. Hollins turns to leave.
Do you think you
I'm sorry. I'm running late. Who's your doctor? Dr. Gibian.
I'll let the nurse know. Walks out. Appreciate it.
Edward tries to sit up but is held in place by the web of tubes running in and out of his body.
Bitch, isn't it? (back to the TV) What are quarks?
Edward studies him.
How long you been here?
Oh, in and out the last few months. Got me on an experimental treatment now. What is a
(after a beat) So, how rough is it?
The chemo? (shrugs) Not too bad. If you don't mind 'round-the-clock vomiting, watching your veins turn black and the feeling that your bones are made of napalm... it's a day at the beach.
(paling slightly) Well that's a relief.
Of course I hear people react differently. You'll know by tonight.
After a beat...
If you don't mind my asking, what's that contraption you got over there? Points over to the high-tech coffee maker. It's a siphon.
Makes coffee.
Fumbles for the nurse's call button.
What else does it do?
What else does it have to do? After another beat...
Did you know that coffee was originally discovered by a shepherd in Ethiopia?
(struggling with the call button) You don't say.
Carter eases himself out of his bed and begins to unravel Edward's call button, while continuing with his story...

It's true. It seems his goats had been eating the berries of an unfamiliar bush. Before long, they were running and jumping all over the place, having just a great old time. The shepherd took some branches to the local monastery where the abbot decided to roast them. When the berries started to burn the beans inside gave off such a pleasant aroma that they brewed them into a stew.
(could care less) Stew, huh? Stew.
(unable to stop himself) So within a few hundred years, it spread to Arabia and Europe. (as Edward stares at him) Even Sumatra, like that hooch you got over there.
It's called Kopi Luwak.
I know what it is.
Never catch me drinking that shit. You do?
You've tried it?
No. I'm more of an instant coffee man.
He hands Edward the liberated call button. Here ya go.
(CONTINUED)
Edward just looks at Carter.
You always had those freckles?
As far as I know. Nice.
My uh... nice.
BEING LIFTED, REVEALING AN APPETIZING ITALIAN MEAL.
INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - NIGHT
As Thomas unpacks aluminum containers from Giorgio Baldi's. Spoons the contents onto a large plate.
Okay, we got prosciutto and melon, some Burata mozzarell', and a veal Paillard. Giorgio sends his best.
You sure you want to eat all that?
That's the plan.
He's cut off as Thomas clears his throat loudly.
(to Carter; off Thomas' look) Oh. Uh, you want Thomas to make you a plate? Tommy, fix a plate for, uh -Carter.
THE BUCKET LIST - Rev. 1/29/07
First name or last?
Interesting. So, you want uh...? Might cheer you up?
Edward pops a piece of fried artichoke in his mouth and chews it loudly. You sure?
Yum, yum... best in
PUKING HIS KIDNEYS OUT.
INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - NIGHT
As his sumptuous dinner is jettisoned to the floor. moaning and spitting. He's Carter never looks up from his book...
(under his breath) Still the best in L.A.? Continues reading as Edward continues purging in the b.g...
INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - NIGHT
Edward is sleeping. The bandages have been removed from his head, which is littered with uneven patches of stubble.
THE BUCKET LIST - Rev. 1/29/07

Sitting up in his bed, Carter looks haggard as he removes an old photo from an envelope. On the back of the photo is written: "DEAR DAD, HERE'S TO GETTING BACK ON YOUR FEET. CAN'T WAIT TO SEE YOU AND MOM DANCING AGAIN. LOVE, RACHEL." He flips it over to see a long-forgotten shot of him and his wife in their twenties, dancing at a sock-hop, laughing, and clutching each other... A lifetime ago...
INT. HOSPITAL BATHROOM - DAY
Edward kneels on the floor, head resting on the toilet seat, fast asleep. He's woken by VOICES coming from the room. He weakly pushes open the door to see who it is.
INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - CONTINUOUS ACTION
ROGER, 47, Carter's oldest son...
Maya made the honor roll again.
Bet your ass she did. That's another fifty I owe her. That child of yours gonna put me in the poor house.
You and me both.
INT. HOSPITAL BATHROOM - DAY
Edward tries to get the taste out of his mouth as he glances down into the bowl. Oh, man.
Wipes his lips and flushes...
INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - CONTINUOUS ACTION
Edward shuffles out, shaking his head.
(to himself) Somewhere, some lucky bastard's having a heart attack right now. Mr. Cole.
Edward gives a small wave without looking up. Gentlemen.
Climbs gingerly into his own bed as Roger hands Carter a small object, badly wrapped with childish wrapping paper.
It's from Kai. Says when he grows up, he wants to be a mechanic like his granddaddy.
Hope you talked him out of that.
Edward watches as Carter peels off the wrapping revealing a keychain with a small, die-cast-'65 Ford Mustang GT pendant.
It's a Shelby 350.
How about that. Always wanted one'a those.
Kai remembered.
He's a good boy.
Mom's worried you're not getting enough rest. Mmm-hmmm.
She loves you, Pop.
(averts his eyes) Mmm-hmmm.
A beat... Roger decides not to pursue it.
Gets ready to
You'll call when you get the test results.
If that day ever comes.
I love you, Pop.
Kisses him goodbye. Roger exits.
Is he your oldest? Yeah.
What's he do?
Roger's a tax attorney.
Carter hands a picture of his immediate family to Edward. He points to Lee.
Who's the little lady?
Rachel's the youngest of the three.
Big age difference.
She was a surprise. We'd hardly gotten the boys out of the house when she came along. Girl can make you cry the way she plays the violin. His swollen pride is evident as Edward hands the picture
back with a polite smile.
(takes a beat) Never stayed married long enough.
That's all right, I been married long enough for the both of us.
And how's that going? It's going.
THE BUCKET LIST - Rev. 1/29/07
That good, huh? Hey, don't get me wrong. I love being married. Been there four times. Problem is I love being single, too. Hard to do them both at the same time. God knows I tried.
Nobody's perfect.
The only successful marriage I had was me and my work. I started making money when I was sixteen, and that was that.
I wanted to be a history professor.
Nobody's perfect.

Made it through two months of City College before Virginia gave me the news... Young, black and broke with a baby on the way, you take the first job they offer you. I always meant to go back. But... Forty-five years goes by pretty fast.
Like smoke through a keyhole.

Carter is sleeping. bathroom. Edward is dragging his I.V. to the

Edward is reading. bathroom. Carter is dragging his I.V. to the

Edward is sleeping. Carter is reading. Nurse Shing replaces Edward's I.V. drip. (Edward makes comment about morphine.)
INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - DAY
Edward is sleeping. Carter. Nurse Shing is drawing blood from
INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - NIGHT
Edward watches TV. After a moment, he turns it OFF. Next to him Carter struggles with pain. Edward feels for him.
INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - DAY (RAIN)
Now the roles are reversed as Carter reads a book and Edward is struggling in pain. Carter looks over in sympathy.
INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - NIGHT
Carter is sleeping. Edward is lying in bed.
I'm so confused. I wonder if I have lost my mind already. Jesus, no, no Jesus I'm not praying. I'm talking to myself. I got nobody to talk to.
Edward gets up out of bed and wanders over to Carter's bed.
Are you awake? Carter, I'm not the only one who farts in his sleep. (after a long pause) I'm scared to death.
THE BUCKET LIST - Rev. 2/2/07 INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - LATE AFTERNOON
Edward and Carter are playing gin.
You know, Nurse Shing could be the Angel of Death. What?
You think you're safe because she likes you better. But maybe she's trying to kill us both, you ever think of that? No. Gin.
(after a beat)
Are you the devil?
INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - NIGHT
Edward is dragging his I.V. to the bathroom.
INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - NIGHT
Carter is dragging his I.V. to the bathroom.
INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - NIGHT
Nurse Shing is giving Carter some pills. Edward some pills. Then she gives
INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - DAY (OVERCAST)
Nurse Shing is replacing Carter's I.V.
INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - NIGHT (RAIN)
Edward and Carter are playing gin. In 1909, Eldwood Baker -(MORE)
THE BUCKET LIST - Rev. 2/2/07

-- of the Knickerbocker Whist Club of New York City was the man generally credited with the innovation and scoring of what has become modern gin rummy. Strangely, Baker achieved more notoriety posthumously as the victim of an unexplained murder. Many suspected at the hand of an irate card player.
You don't say.
INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - LATE AFTERNOON
Edward and Carter walk down the hallway together, dragging their I.V.'s.
Ever think about suicide? Suicide?
Lord, no. Thought so. What?
Stage one.
The five stages.
Oh, denial, anger, bargaining, depression, acceptance.
Of course you're not thinking of suicide. You're in stage one. Denial.
What stage are you in?
THE BUCKET LIST - Rev. 2/2/07
And thinking of suicide.
As Edward and Carter walk away from us, we HEAR:
You ever see The Defiant Ones?
INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - DAY
Edward is having some blood drawn.
Can't you use the old blood?
INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - DAY (OVERCAST)
Edward is dragging his I.V. to the bathroom.
INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - DAY (LATE AFTERNOON)
Carter is dragging his I.V. to the bathroom.
THE BUCKET LIST - Rev. 2/1/07 INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - NIGHT
Edward, dragging his I.V., comes out of the bathroom. passes Carter, dragging his I.V. on his way into the bathroom. They perfunctorily high-five each other.
INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - AN EMPTY I.V. BOTTLE - DAY
Edward is reading a Milton Friedman book. Nurse Shing removes the empty bottle from the stand next to Carter's bed.
THE BUCKET LIST - Rev. 1/23/07
I'll take a clean bill of health if you got one.
THE BUCKET LIST - Rev. 2/6/07
She heads out as he checks his watch.
(to Edward) Mind if I turn this on?
Edward waves his hand dismissively. Carter dons a pair of headphones and tunes the television to "Jeopardy" and becomes immediately engrossed...
What is The Great Divide?... What is somnambulism?...
Edward puts his book down. Glances over at Carter...
What is the Hindenburg?
You ever miss one?
(lifts one headphone off his ear) What's that?
You ever miss one?
(thinks a moment) Oh, sure. Missed one last week. I mixed up optometrist with ophthalmologist.
That can happen. (a beat) Ever think about going on the show?
Ahh, that's for other people. What is a banyan tree?
Edward studies him for a moment, then...
INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - NIGHT
THE SOUNDS OF A BASEBALL GAME. Carter writes on a sheet of lined paper resting between the open pages of his book. We see the words: INSERT - WORDS BUCKET LIST WITNESS SOMETHING TRULY MAJESTIC.
Hit the cutoff, for Christ sake! See, that's the problem with the game today.
BACK TO SCENE

Carter lowers the book just enough to reveal Edward watching TV on his stomach with his head propped on a pillow at the foot of his bed, stretching his tubes and cables to the limit as he berates the Dodgers.
No fundamentals. Did you ever read The Time of Your Life?
By William Saroyan?
Yeah: 'No foundation all the way down the line.' When we were growing up... What are you doing?
Nothing.
Just scribbling.
Scribbling what? (diverted by the TV) Yeah, that's what ya wanna do, bounce a slider with a man on third! You see these kids today... Ah, I'm talking to myself.

Carter puts on a pair of headphones and listens to BACH'S CELLO SUITE NO. 1 in G-MAJOR. He returns to his list. He thinks a moment before writing the words: "HELP A COMPLETE STRANGER FOR THE GOOD." When he looks up again, Hollins is standing at the foot of Edward's bed. The doctor's face is pinched and somber as he speaks... Edward listens implacably, his face impossible to read. Carter slides the headphones off to listen...
Six months. A year if we're lucky. There's an experimental program we're conducting, and, though I don't want to get your hopes up, I think you'd be an
-- candidate. It's cutting edge medicine. The odds are against you, but -Hey, Doc.
DR. HOLLINS Yes?
You're blocking my view. Oh.
DR. HOLLINS Hollins steps to the side of the television. a beat. Stares for
Well, if you have any questions.
Waits another beat then starts for the door.
(eyes on the game) One thing. Yes.
Yeah?
'Something you wanted to ask Dr. Hollins?
I'm sorry, I'm not familiar with Mister Chambers' -EDWARD Well, get familiar.
Hollins stares at him but Edward's attention never leaves the screen.
Just want to know where I stand is all.
Hollins nods...
Let me get your chart.

He walks out, leaving Carter staring at Edward's back. ANGLE - EDWARD WATCHING THE GAME. Slowly his eyes fall from the screen as he contemplates the news of the end of his life. Carter searches for the right words...
Edward doesn't respond.
A beat...
There was a survey once; a thousand people were asked, if they could know in advance, would they want to know the exact day of their death.
Hollins enters reading from the chart as he starts to
Ninety-six percent of them said no.
Carter listens implacably...
I always kinda leaned toward the other four percent. I thought it would be liberating, knowing how much time you had left to work with.
He crumples the list he was working on into a ball...
It turns out it's not.
He looks up at Edward who is staring back at him over his shoulder... They hold the look for several moments. Finally...
You want to play cards?
Thought you'd never ask.
INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - DAY

CLOSE ON EDWARD fast asleep. A square of sunlight moves across his eyes. The light is cut off as someone moves past and begins cleaning up the mess of tissues and pill bottles on the bedside table. The ray of light stutters until Edward opens his eyes to see Thomas straightening up his side of the room.
Rise and shine.
Edward, without even opening his eyes, gives Thomas the finger.

Edward rolls over to see Carter, sleeping with his back to him. He notices the crumpled ball of paper and catalogue on the floor between them. With some effort, he leans out of bed to reach for it. I'll get it.
Thomas hands Edward the paper.
Thomas, call Lisa at Sotheby's and tell her I won't be bidding this season.
I understand. Sir, I don't mean to be indelicate, but how do you want me to deal with your uh...
Death? Treat it as if it were your own. Now go get me one of those almond croissants and don't buy any green bananas.
Then Thomas exits. Edward unravels it to reveal Carter's "Bucket List." Edward scans the list of activities, scoffing under his breath.
What are you doing? What is this? Give it back. What is it? Give it back.
EDWARD
It was on the floor. I didn't know it was a State secret.
(sighs) My freshman philosophy professor assigned this exercise in forward thinking. We had to make a list of all the things we dreamed of doing with our lives before we...
